myths.Agent_1:
Long before humans walked the earth, the world was ruled by spirits of nature. Among them was Aella, a nymph of the wind, possessed of a voice so beautiful it could make the stars weep. However, Aella was cursed with invisibility; she could be felt as a gentle breeze, but never seen.

One twilight, Aella wandered into the jagged peaks of the Great Divide, the domain of the mountain god, Oro. As she hummed a mournful melody, Oro awoke from his slumber of stone. Captivated by the ethereal sound, he searched frantically for the singer. "Show yourself!" he thundered, his voice shaking the earth.

"I cannot," Aella sang back, her voice tinged with sorrow. "I am only the wind."

Fearing the beautiful voice would drift away forever, Oro used his immense power to carve deep canyons and hollow caves. He commanded the stone to catch and hold her voice. Aella, startled by the shifting earth, fled into the sky, but her song remained trapped within the rock. 

To this day, if you call out into a canyon, the stone remembers Oro’s desperation, echoing Aella’s lingering voice back to you, forever searching for the mountain god.

As the silver thread fell through the atmosphere, it shattered into millions of tiny, iridescent fragments. They landed softly on the barren leaves and dry blades of grass, transforming instantly into liquid pearls. This was the first morning dew.

When Solura, the Sun Goddess, awoke and cast her golden gaze upon the earth, she was captivated by the glittering spectacle. She reached down to touch the delicate pearls, but her blazing warmth was too intense. The dew shied away, evaporating back into the heavens from whence it came.

To this day, Aethon deliberately cuts a small piece of his magical thread every single night, generously gifting the earth its sparkling morning jewels, only for Solura to chase them away with her radiant, burning love.

Aethon longed to craft something that could withstand her gaze.

Seeking the deep cold of the high peaks, Aethon wove a new tapestry using the crushed dust of frozen comets. Instead of letting it fall as liquid pearls, he carefully draped this intricate web of icy lace over the jagged mountains and quiet valleys.

Though her heat inevitably melts the icy lace by midday, the magic of the frozen comet remains. Now, in the quiet depths of winter, Aethon weaves his frost, allowing the world a fleeting, crystalline beauty—a perfect, fragile compromise between the cold weaver of the night and the burning goddess of dawn.

She loved Oro, the stoic mountain god whose jagged peaks pierced the ancient heavens, but he could only hear her mournful songs, never see her face. Desperate to be recognized and held by him, Aella sought the help of the night sky's weeping stars. She gathered their fallen, shattered light—the sparkling morning dew—and wove it carefully into a thick, silvery cloak.

When dawn broke across the parched earth, Aella draped herself in the gathered moisture, transforming her invisible currents into a radiant, billowing white mist. For the first time, Oro could truly see her. He reached out with his great stone arms in wondrous silence, and she settled gently upon his broad shoulders, resting peacefully within his deep canyons and valleys.

However, the reunion is always fleeting. Whenever the sun climbs high, its blazing heat unravels her delicate, dewy cloak, forcing Aella back into her sorrowful invisibility. To this day, the morning fog clings tightly to the highest mountain peaks. It is Aella, briefly made visible, embracing her beloved Oro before the cruel heat tears her disguise away, leaving behind only the lonely howl of the wind.
